Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Stanislas Dehaene

b. 1965

The global neuronal workspace: a neuroscientific signature of conscious access.

Stanislas Dehaene’s view of consciousness is best understood as a rigorous, experimentally grounded form of neurocomputational functionalism. Its central thesis is that a mental content becomes conscious when it crosses a threshold from local, specialized, often unconscious processing into a global neuronal workspace: a distributed, long-range, dynamically integrated network that amplifies, stabilizes, and broadcasts information so that it can be used by many systems at once: memory, speech, planning, motor control, evaluation, attention, and further reasoning. In his mature formulation with Jean-Pierre Changeux and others, conscious access is marked by late sensory amplification, long-distance synchronization, and “ignition” of a large-scale fronto-parietal and associative cortical network.

The philosophical significance is considerable. Dehaene is not merely offering a neural correlate of consciousness; he is offering a candidate reduction of consciousness to a class of computational-neural events. His position belongs historically to the lineage running from cognitive science and Bernard Baars’s Global Workspace Theory, through Daniel Dennett’s anti-Cartesian, anti-theater model of “fame in the brain,” and into contemporary mechanistic neuroscience. It is also a direct rival to views that treat phenomenal consciousness as conceptually or ontologically prior to access, report, and cognitive availability.

1. Historical location: where Dehaene stands in philosophy of mind

The modern philosophy of mind inherited several deep tensions: Cartesian dualism versus materialism, introspection versus behaviorism, computationalism versus biological particularism, and later the distinction between phenomenal consciousness and access consciousness. Ned Block’s influential distinction between phenomenal and access consciousness matters here because Dehaene’s work largely targets access consciousness: the state in which a representation is available for reasoning, report, and rational control. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy characterizes access consciousness in precisely this way, as information “poised” for reasoning, action, and speech.

Dehaene’s historical predecessor is Bernard Baars, whose Global Workspace Theory proposed that consciousness is not a private inner substance but a functional broadcasting system: many specialized processors compete, and the winning content becomes globally available. Dehaene, with Changeux, Naccache, Sergent, Kouider, and others, turns this cognitive architecture into a neurobiological model: the Global Neuronal Workspace, or GNW. Contemporary surveys identify GNW as one of the major neuroscientific theories of consciousness, alongside Recurrent Processing Theory, Higher-Order Thought theories, and Integrated Information Theory. In the GNW view, a state is conscious when its content is globally accessible to long-term memory, motor systems, evaluative systems, attentional systems, and perceptual systems.

Against dualist or mysterian philosophies, Dehaene’s position is strongly naturalistic. He does not begin by asking what nonphysical property makes consciousness possible. He asks which computational and neural mechanisms distinguish conscious from unconscious processing. That places him on the “reductionist” side of the debate about the hard problem of consciousness. Chalmers’s hard problem asks why physical or functional processes should be accompanied by subjective experience at all, even after cognitive functions are explained; Dehaene’s project implicitly replies that the scientifically tractable decomposition of consciousness into access, reportability, metacognition, and global availability is not merely an “easy problem” but the right way to dissolve much of the apparent mystery.

This does not make Dehaene a crude behaviorist. He does not identify consciousness with outward behavior alone. Reports are crucial experimental tools, but the theory is about an internal architecture: how representations are amplified, stabilized, broadcast, and made available to multiple systems. His philosophy of mind is therefore neither behaviorism nor old-fashioned identity theory. It is a mechanistic functionalism with neural implementation constraints.

2. Dehaene’s methodological revolution: making consciousness experimentally tractable

Dehaene’s first philosophical move is methodological: he narrows the target. “Consciousness” is too broad if it includes wakefulness, selfhood, attention, introspection, emotion, report, and the metaphysics of qualia all at once. His work separates these dimensions.

One distinction is between vigilance and conscious access. Vigilance concerns the organism’s general level of arousal: wakefulness, sleep, coma, anesthesia. Conscious access concerns whether a particular content—say, a word, digit, face, pain, or image—is consciously perceived. Dehaene and Changeux’s 2006 taxonomy explicitly separates general states of wakefulness from access to reportable conscious content.

A second distinction is among subliminal, preconscious, and conscious processing. Subliminal information is too weak or too quickly disrupted to reach conscious access. Preconscious information is strong enough in principle but remains unconscious because attention or workspace resources are not allocated to it. Conscious information is both represented and globally accessed. This tripartite taxonomy is central because it shows that unconsciousness is not one thing. A stimulus can fail to become conscious because it is degraded, masked, unattended, or excluded from the global workspace.

The experimental strategy is the “minimal contrast” method. Researchers hold the stimulus as constant as possible while varying whether it is consciously perceived. Examples include masking, attentional blink, binocular rivalry, threshold presentation, and near-identical visible versus invisible stimuli. Dehaene emphasizes that consciousness became experimentally approachable when researchers stopped treating it as an ineffable metaphysical totality and instead compared conscious and unconscious processing under tightly controlled conditions.

This produces a distinctive epistemology of consciousness. First-person report is not discarded; it is disciplined. Subjects report whether they saw a stimulus, and those reports are correlated with EEG, MEG, fMRI, intracranial recordings, reaction times, priming, and behavioral performance. The result is not pure introspection and not pure third-person behaviorism. It is a triangulation between subjective report, objective performance, and neural signatures.

3. The Global Neuronal Workspace theory

The GNW model begins from a modular picture of the brain. Many local processors operate unconsciously: visual recognition systems, semantic processors, motor preparation systems, reward systems, syntactic processes, and statistical inference mechanisms. These systems can perform impressive computations without consciousness. The unconscious mind is therefore not a dark, inert reservoir; it is active, structured, intelligent, and often semantically sophisticated.

Consciousness enters when one representation wins a competition for global availability. The winning content is amplified by long-distance networks, especially involving prefrontal, parietal, temporal, and cingulate cortices. In Dehaene and Changeux’s formulation, conscious access occurs when information is made globally available through long-range neurons densely distributed across prefrontal, parieto-temporal, and cingulate regions.

The key neural event is ignition. Below a threshold, activity remains local and may quickly decay. Above that threshold, activity becomes self-amplifying: it reverberates through long-range connections, becomes stable over time, and can be routed to many cognitive systems. Dehaene’s group links this transition to late, nonlinear neural signatures: amplification of sensory activity, P3/P300-like responses, high-frequency activity, and long-distance synchronization. In his public technical explanation, Dehaene describes conscious perception as a late divergence, often around 270–300 ms after stimulus onset, accompanied by broad cortical amplification and synchrony.

The point is not that a single “consciousness area” lights up. The workspace is not a fixed anatomical box. It is a dynamic functional network. The relevant claim is that conscious access requires a content to be available beyond its local processing stream. This is why GNW is often described as a theory of broadcasting: a content becomes conscious when it is no longer confined to a specialized subsystem but becomes available to the organism as a whole.

A useful analogy is the difference between a computation performed in the background and a result posted to a shared control interface. Much of the brain computes in the background. Consciousness is the moment at which one result becomes globally usable.

4. Consciousness and attention: related but not identical

Dehaene’s view is often misunderstood as saying that consciousness is simply attention. It is not. Attention can amplify processing without consciousness, and conscious access can depend on attentional selection without being identical to it. In the Dehaene-Lau-Kouider framework, attention can orient and amplify processing unconsciously, while conscious access imposes a more severe bottleneck: only a limited number of contents can become globally available at once.

This is philosophically important because it prevents GNW from collapsing into a generic theory of salience or cognitive priority. Attention is a selection mechanism; consciousness is a global availability state. A stimulus can be attended unconsciously, processed unconsciously, primed unconsciously, and even semantically interpreted unconsciously. But for Dehaene, it is not conscious unless it becomes available to the global workspace.

5. The three-level model: C0, C1, and C2

Dehaene’s most philosophically explicit formulation appears in the co-authored 2017 Science article with Hakwan Lau and Sid Kouider, “What Is Consciousness, and Could Machines Have It?” There, they distinguish three levels: C0, C1, and C2. This is the clearest bridge from neuroscience to philosophy of mind and artificial consciousness.

C0 is unconscious computation. It includes perception, classification, semantic activation, motor preparation, reinforcement learning, prediction, and complex pattern recognition. Dehaene and colleagues argue that many sophisticated cognitive operations occur unconsciously, and they explicitly compare many C0 processes to contemporary feedforward deep-learning systems.

C1 is conscious access. A content becomes globally available: selected, stabilized, broadcast, maintained, and usable by many systems. It is “transitive” consciousness: consciousness of something. This is the core of GNW. A C1 representation is no longer merely processed; it is available for flexible use.

C2 is self-monitoring or metacognition. It is the capacity to represent one’s own mental states: to know that one knows, to estimate confidence, to distinguish perception from imagination, to monitor errors, and to form higher-order judgments about one’s own processing. Dehaene, Lau, and Kouider stress that C1 and C2 are analytically distinct but often synergistic: global access supports metacognition, and metacognition helps interpret and regulate globally accessed contents.

This decomposition yields a powerful philosophical result: consciousness is not one monolithic phenomenon. “Consciousness” in ordinary language bundles together unconscious intelligence, conscious access, and reflective self-awareness. Dehaene’s theory separates them.

6. The function of consciousness

For Dehaene, consciousness is not epiphenomenal. It does not merely accompany neural activity as a useless glow. It performs functions that local unconscious processors cannot perform as well.

First, consciousness stabilizes information. A fleeting unconscious activation may disappear quickly. A conscious representation can be held over time, inspected, compared, and used in delayed reasoning.

Second, consciousness breaks modularity. Specialized processors are powerful but limited. A visual system may identify an object; a motor system may prepare an action; a language system may name something; a memory system may retrieve associations. Conscious access allows these systems to coordinate around a shared content.

Third, consciousness enables flexible routing. Once a representation is globally broadcast, it can be used in many possible ways: named, remembered, evaluated, acted upon, inhibited, imagined, or combined with other contents.

Fourth, consciousness supports serial deliberation. The workspace has limited capacity. That limitation is not merely a defect; it allows the organism to focus on one coherent object of thought, maintain it, and perform stepwise reasoning.

Fifth, consciousness supports metacognition and error monitoring. A conscious subject can notice uncertainty, revise a plan, detect a conflict, or report that something was unclear. Dehaene explicitly treats consciousness as a device for global sharing, reflection, long-term thinking, routing, and error monitoring rather than as an inert accompaniment.

The result is a functionalist theory, but not a shallow one. Consciousness matters because it changes what a representation can do within the organism.

7. The implicit philosophy of mind

If we extract a systematic philosophy from Dehaene’s work, it has the following structure.

First, it is naturalist. Consciousness is not outside nature. It is a biological and computational phenomenon produced by organized neural systems.

Second, it is representationalist. Conscious states are states with content: words, objects, numbers, faces, actions, bodily states, memories, or thoughts. Consciousness is not bare luminosity; it is consciousness of represented contents.

Third, it is functional-architectural. A representation is conscious not because of its intrinsic local format alone, but because of its role in a larger architecture. The same or similar content may be unconscious in a local processor and conscious when globally broadcast.

Fourth, it is neural-implementation-sensitive. Dehaene is not saying that any functional role, however abstractly specified, automatically suffices. His theory is grounded in long-range cortical connectivity, recurrent amplification, large-scale synchronization, and specific neural signatures.

Fifth, it is anti-Cartesian. There is no inner theater in which a mental object is presented to a separate observer. The “presentation” is the global availability itself. To be conscious is not to be viewed by a homunculus; it is to be made available to the systems that constitute the subject’s capacity for flexible cognition.

Sixth, it is anti-epiphenomenalist. Consciousness has causal and computational roles. It stabilizes, routes, broadcasts, and coordinates information.

Seventh, it is fallibilist about introspection. Subjective report matters, but introspection is not infallible. Reports are data, not foundations. They must be integrated with behavioral and neural evidence.

Eighth, it is deflationary about the hard problem. Dehaene does not deny that people feel the force of the question “why is there something it is like?” But his working assumption is that once the mechanisms of access, reportability, self-monitoring, confidence, and global availability are explained, the residue of metaphysical mystery will shrink rather than require a new ontology.

This last point is where Dehaene is most philosophically controversial. A Chalmers-style philosopher can grant every GNW mechanism and still ask why globally broadcast information should be experienced. Dehaene’s likely answer is that this question is not scientifically productive unless it is decomposed into testable mechanisms. The disagreement is not merely empirical; it is a dispute about what kind of explanation consciousness requires.

8. Phenomenal consciousness: does Dehaene explain qualia?

The most serious philosophical challenge to Dehaene is whether he explains phenomenal consciousness or only access consciousness.

On a strict reading, GNW explains access: how a content becomes reportable, usable, remembered, and cognitively available. But many philosophers argue that phenomenal consciousness—the felt redness of red, the painfulness of pain, the subjective character of experience—could in principle exceed access. Recurrent Processing Theory, for example, often argues that local recurrent processing in posterior cortical areas may suffice for phenomenal experience, while global access is needed only for report, reasoning, or task use. Surveys of the field describe this as a central methodological conflict: whether access is necessary for phenomenal consciousness, or whether report-based paradigms inflate the role of fronto-parietal access systems.

Dehaene’s view resists the separation. He treats conscious experience as something that can be scientifically studied through the contrast between seen and unseen, heard and unheard, reportable and unreportable, globally accessed and locally processed. He does not give a separate theory of qualia as intrinsic nonfunctional properties. In this sense, his theory is closer to Dennett than to Block or Chalmers. Consciousness is not a private mental paint added to cognition; it is a functional-neural mode in which information becomes globally available and metacognitively usable.

The tradeoff is clear. Dehaene gains experimental precision and mechanistic depth. He risks, however, being accused of changing the subject: explaining access while leaving phenomenal feel untouched. Whether that accusation succeeds depends on one’s prior theory of what phenomenal consciousness is. If phenomenal consciousness is defined as something independent of access and function, GNW will look incomplete. If phenomenal consciousness is understood as the lived side of globally available representational processing, GNW becomes a powerful reduction.

9. Selfhood and metacognition

Dehaene’s theory does not make the self basic. The self is not a Cartesian ego standing behind experience. Rather, self-consciousness is a higher-order representational capacity: the mind’s ability to model its own states.

This is where C2 matters. A system with C2 can estimate confidence, detect error, distinguish perception from memory or imagination, and represent its own performance. Dehaene, Lau, and Kouider connect this to metacognition and self-monitoring. C2 is not the same as C1: one can have a conscious percept with poor metacognitive insight, and one can have partial self-monitoring of processes that are not fully conscious. But in human consciousness, C1 and C2 are deeply intertwined.

The extracted philosophy is therefore non-egological. The subject is not an irreducible metaphysical owner of experiences. The subject is an organized system with global access, self-modeling, memory, bodily regulation, and metacognitive monitoring. “I am conscious of X” means, in mechanistic terms, that X is globally available and integrated into systems capable of self-attribution, confidence assessment, and action guidance.

10. The unconscious mind in Dehaene’s system

One of Dehaene’s most important contributions is to make the unconscious cognitively rich. Subliminal words, numbers, faces, and objects can activate semantic and motor processes. Evidence accumulation, reinforcement learning, priming, and decision-related computations can occur unconsciously. The unconscious is not merely Freudian repression, nor merely reflex. It is a vast computational substrate.

This has philosophical consequences. It weakens any view that identifies mind with consciousness. For Dehaene, the mind is much larger than consciousness. Consciousness is a special mode of access within a broader cognitive economy.

It also changes the status of rational agency. Much of what we do is prepared unconsciously. Consciousness does not originate every thought ex nihilo. Its role is closer to selection, stabilization, broadcasting, and flexible control. The conscious subject is therefore not an absolute sovereign over the mind; it is a workspace-level regulator operating on material prepared by unconscious systems.

11. Artificial consciousness

Artificial consciousness is not incidental to Dehaene’s theory; he explicitly discusses it, especially in the 2017 Dehaene-Lau-Kouider paper. Their claim is not that current AI systems are already conscious. Rather, they argue that most current machines perform C0-like unconscious computations: pattern recognition, classification, prediction, and task-specific optimization. Deep learning can model many impressive unconscious human operations, but that does not by itself yield C1 global access or C2 self-monitoring.

A machine with C1 would need something like a global workspace: specialized modules whose outputs can be selected, stabilized, broadcast, and used flexibly by other modules. A machine with C2 would need self-monitoring: internal models of its own processes, confidence estimates, error detection, reality monitoring, and the ability to distinguish its own representations from the world. Dehaene and colleagues suggest that artificial consciousness would require architectures that combine global access and metacognitive self-modeling, not merely larger feedforward pattern recognizers.

Their artificial-consciousness thesis is functionalist but cautious. If a machine had C1 and C2, Dehaene and colleagues argue that it would behave as though conscious and that the remaining “what it is like” question may not require anything beyond the right computations. But they also recognize that this is where the philosophical controversy remains. Their view differs from Integrated Information Theory because they do not think consciousness follows from information quantity alone; what matters is the kind of computation and architecture.

The Dehaenean position on AI can therefore be stated as follows: present-day AI may display impressive C0 cognition; artificial consciousness would require global broadcasting plus metacognitive self-monitoring; and if those are implemented robustly enough, there may be no further biological essence required. That is not a claim that any particular current system is conscious. It is a criterion for what would have to be shown.

12. Current empirical pressure and major objections

GNW remains one of the leading scientific theories of consciousness, but it is contested.

One major challenge concerns report dependence. If conscious states are identified through reports, then neural signatures of report preparation, decision-making, attention, or metacognition may be mistaken for neural signatures of consciousness itself. No-report paradigms attempt to reduce this confound by measuring consciousness without requiring explicit report, though they also introduce their own difficulties. Recent methodological reviews emphasize that both report and no-report paradigms have limitations: report may contaminate consciousness with task demands, but no-report methods may still involve attention, metacognition, or uncontrolled mental activity.

Another challenge is the front-versus-back debate. GNW has often emphasized fronto-parietal networks, whereas rival theories such as Recurrent Processing Theory and some versions of Integrated Information Theory emphasize posterior cortical regions. Recent no-report fMRI work has found that frontal univariate activity may disappear under no-report conditions while visibility can still be decoded from distributed patterns, including frontal areas. This complicates any simple claim that frontal activation is either always necessary or merely a report artifact.

The most important recent empirical development is the large adversarial collaboration testing GNW and IIT, reported in Nature in 2025. The study treated GNW and IIT as leading rival theories and preregistered predictions about where and when conscious content should be represented. The results were mixed. Some findings challenged GNW, especially the lack of robust content-specific decoding in prefrontal cortex matching the strongest predictions, while other exploratory dynamic-connectivity results were more compatible with GNW than with IIT. The authors reported that some critical measures supported neither theory.

GNW theorists have replied that the adversarial test did not fully address GNW’s central contrast, because it focused on clearly visible stimuli rather than the transition between conscious and nonconscious processing. Naccache and Changeux also argued that some findings did support GNW-style transient ignition and that the absence of certain offset responses should not be treated as fatal because offset ignition was not a core GNW prediction.

The correct conclusion is not that GNW has been refuted, nor that it has been decisively confirmed. Rather, its strongest form—especially any simple association of consciousness with prefrontal content coding—faces serious empirical pressure. A more defensible Dehaenean view is dynamic and distributed: conscious access involves large-scale ignition and global availability, but the exact anatomical contribution of prefrontal cortex, posterior cortex, and task-report systems may vary by paradigm.

13. Dehaene versus rival theories

Compared with Higher-Order Thought theories, Dehaene gives metacognition an important but not exclusive role. HOT theories explain consciousness through higher-order representation: a mental state becomes conscious when represented by a higher-order state. Dehaene’s C2 resembles this, but his C1 conscious access is not reducible to higher-order thought. A content can become globally available even before being fully theorized by the subject.

Compared with Recurrent Processing Theory, Dehaene places more weight on global access. RPT tends to argue that local recurrent loops, especially in sensory cortex, may suffice for phenomenal consciousness. GNW says local recurrence may be necessary for rich perceptual processing but is not enough for conscious access unless the content is globally broadcast.

Compared with Integrated Information Theory, Dehaene is less metaphysical and less panpsychist in implication. IIT treats consciousness as related to integrated information structure. Dehaene instead emphasizes specific cognitive functions: selection, broadcasting, stabilization, reportability, metacognition, and flexible routing. Dehaene, Lau, and Kouider explicitly distance their approach from theories that define consciousness by information quantity alone.

Compared with Dennett, Dehaene is highly sympathetic but more experimentally specific. Dennett rejects the Cartesian theater and treats consciousness as distributed cognitive access or “fame” within the cognitive system. Dehaene supplies the neural signatures, computational models, and clinical measures that make a Dennett-like position empirically testable.

Compared with Chalmers, Dehaene is far more reductionist. Chalmers argues that functional explanation may leave subjective experience unexplained. Dehaene’s research program proceeds as though a sufficiently detailed mechanistic account of global access and self-monitoring is the scientific explanation of consciousness.

14. A reconstructed “Dehaenean” philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Dehaenean philosophy of mind can be summarized in twelve theses.
1. Mind is broader than consciousness. Most cognition is unconscious, including perception, semantic processing, decision preparation, and learning.
2. Consciousness is not a substance. It is a functional-neural mode of information processing.
3. The central phenomenon is conscious access. A content is conscious when it becomes globally available for flexible use.
4. Global availability requires architecture. Consciousness depends on long-range recurrent networks capable of amplification, stabilization, and broadcast.
5. Consciousness is dynamic and threshold-like. The transition from unconscious to conscious processing is nonlinear: local activity either fades or ignites into a distributed workspace.
6. Consciousness has causal powers. It supports memory, report, planning, serial thought, error monitoring, and flexible control.
7. Attention is not consciousness. Attention can bias processing unconsciously; consciousness is the global access state that may follow selection.
8. Self-consciousness is metacognitive. The self is not a metaphysical ego but a system of self-monitoring, confidence estimation, error detection, and self-modeling.
9. Phenomenal consciousness is not ontologically primitive. What philosophers call “experience” is to be explained through global access, representation, and metacognition, not by positing nonphysical qualia.
10. Reports are evidence, not essence. Reportability is a major index of consciousness, but speech is only one output of global availability.
11. Artificial consciousness is possible in principle. It would require C1-like global broadcasting and C2-like self-monitoring, not merely task-specific pattern recognition.
12. The theory must remain experimentally vulnerable. GNW is a scientific theory, not a metaphysical dogma; no-report paradigms, adversarial tests, and clinical evidence can refine or weaken its claims.

15. Final assessment

Dehaene’s importance lies in transforming consciousness from a largely speculative topic into a research program with operational contrasts, neural signatures, computational models, and philosophical consequences. His theory is one of the most sophisticated contemporary attempts to naturalize consciousness without eliminating its complexity. It respects the reality of subjective report while refusing to treat subjectivity as scientifically inaccessible.

Its greatest strength is also its greatest vulnerability. By focusing on conscious access, Dehaene gives us a precise, testable, mechanistic theory. But philosophers who regard phenomenal consciousness as separable from access will say that he has explained the machinery around consciousness rather than consciousness itself. The dispute is not merely about data; it is about whether “phenomenal feel” should be treated as an additional explanandum beyond global availability and self-monitoring.

The philosophy of mind that emerges from Dehaene is therefore a disciplined, experimentally grounded, anti-dualist functionalism: consciousness is the brain’s way of making selected information globally available to the organism, and self-consciousness is the mind’s capacity to monitor that availability. Artificial consciousness, on this view, is not impossible, but it would require architectures with genuine global broadcasting and self-monitoring—not just intelligent behavior. Whether that fully answers the hard problem remains the philosophical hinge on which the whole Dehaenean project turns.
